messages.content:
A statistician walks into a room and wants to
know how likely it is that someone in the room shares a birthday with HER
specifically (not with each other — with her).

Assume 365 equally likely birthdays, ignore leap years, and that she knows
her own birthday. What is the minimum number of OTHER people that must be in
the room so that the probability at least one of them shares HER birthday
exceeds 50%?

Think carefully about exactly which event is being asked about.


When you are finished, output your final answer on its own line, as the last line of your response, in the exact format:
ANSWER: <your answer>

text:
The statistician has a fixed birthday. For each of the \( n \) other people in the room, the probability of not sharing her birthday is \( \frac{364}{365} \). Assuming independence, the probability that none of the \( n \) people share her birthday is \( \left(\frac{364}{365}\right)^n \). We want the probability that at least one shares her birthday to exceed 50%, i.e.,  
\[
1 - \left(\frac{364}{365}\right)^n > 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{364}{365}\right)^n < 0.5.
\]  
Taking natural logarithms:  
\[
n \ln\left(\frac{364}{365}\right) < \ln(0.5) \quad \Rightarrow \quad n > \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(364) - \ln(365)}.
\]  
Using \(\ln(0.5) \approx -0.693147\) and \(\ln(364/365) \approx -0.0027435\), we get \( n > 252.65 \). Thus the smallest integer \( n \) satisfying the inequality is 253. For \( n = 253 \), the probability is approximately \( 1 - (364/365)^{253} \approx 0.5002 > 0.5 \).

ANSWER: 253
